topics_flat: Discrete Mathematics > Combinatorics > Coloring schemes, extremal arguments
Number Theory > Divisibility / Factorization > Prime numbers
Number Theory > Number-Theoretic Functions > τ (number of divisors)
prompt: For a positive integer $r \ge 2$ each positive integer is coloured into one of $r$ colours. Suppose that for each integer number $n$ and each pair $(a, b)$ of two colours $a$ and $b$ the difference between the number of divisors of $n$ coloured $a$ and the number of divisors of $n$ coloured $b$ is at most $1$. Find all possible values of $r$.
response_text:
Let \(d(n)\) be the number of positive divisors of \(n\).  
For each \(n\), let \(c_i(n)\) be the number of divisors of \(n\) having colour \(i\), for \(i=1,\dots,r\).

The condition says that for every \(n\) and every pair of colours \(a,b\),
\[
|c_a(n)-c_b(n)|\le 1.
\]
So for every \(n\), the divisors of \(n\) are distributed among the \(r\) colours as evenly as possible.

We must determine all \(r\ge 2\) for which such a colouring of the positive integers exists.

---

## Step 1: A necessary divisibility condition

If among \(r\) integers the pairwise differences are at most \(1\), then each number is either
\[
\left\lfloor \frac{d(n)}r\right\rfloor \quad\text{or}\quad \left\lceil \frac{d(n)}r\right\rceil.
\]
In particular, if \(r>d(n)\), then some colours occur \(0\) times and some \(1\) time.

Now look at special \(n\).

### For \(n=p^{r-1}\)
where \(p\) is prime, we have
\[
d(n)=r.
\]
Thus each colour must appear exactly once among the divisors
\[
1,p,p^2,\dots,p^{r-1}.
\]
So along every chain of powers of a prime, the \(r\) divisors up to exponent \(r-1\) use all \(r\) colours exactly once.

### For \(n=p^r\)
we have \(d(n)=r+1\). Hence among
\[
1,p,p^2,\dots,p^r
\]
the colour counts must be \(2,1,1,\dots,1\): exactly one colour appears twice and the others once.

Since for \(p^{r-1}\) every colour already appears once among \(1,p,\dots,p^{r-1}\), it follows that the extra divisor \(p^r\) must have the same colour as \(1\).

So for every prime \(p\),
\[
\operatorname{col}(p^r)=\operatorname{col}(1).
\]

Now repeat with \(p^{2r-1}\): among
\[
1,p,\dots,p^{2r-1}
\]
there are \(2r\) divisors, so each colour must appear exactly twice. Since the first \(r\) divisors already use each colour once, the next block
\[
p^r,p^{r+1},\dots,p^{2r-1}
\]
must also use each colour once. In particular \(p^{2r}\) again has the colour of \(1\).

Continuing, we get:

> For every prime \(p\), the colours of \(1,p,p^2,\dots\) are periodic modulo \(r\), and in every block of length \(r\) each colour appears exactly once.

---

## Step 2: Use numbers of the form \(pq\)

Let the colour of \(1\) be \(c_0\).

Take two distinct primes \(p,q\). The divisors of \(pq\) are
\[
1,p,q,pq.
\]
So \(d(pq)=4\).

If \(r\ge 5\), then distributing 4 objects among \(r\ge 5\) colours with pairwise difference at most \(1\) is impossible, because some colours would occur \(0\) times and some \(1\) time, but then two colours both with \(0\) and \(1\) are fine; however if \(r>4\), actually all counts are \(0\) or \(1\), so this is possible only if there are at least 4 colours used. So this does not yet rule out \(r\ge 5\).

Instead use \(n=pq\) together with the periodicity.

Since \(1,p,\dots,p^{r-1}\) use all colours once, the colour of \(p\) can be arbitrary, but fixed relative to \(1\). Similarly for \(q\).

Now examine \(n=p^{r-1}q\). It has
\[
d(n)=2r
\]
divisors. Hence each colour must appear exactly \(2\) times.

These divisors are
\[
1,p,\dots,p^{r-1},\quad q,pq,\dots,p^{r-1}q.
\]
The first row already contains each colour once. Therefore the second row must also contain each colour once.

Thus multiplication by \(q\) permutes the colours on the set
\[
\{1,p,\dots,p^{r-1}\}.
\]
In particular, for every prime \(q\), the map
\[
x \mapsto xq
\]
acts as a permutation of colours on any full \(r\)-block of powers of \(p\).

This means the colour of an integer depends additively on prime exponents modulo \(r\). More concretely, after relabelling colours by elements of \(\mathbb Z/r\mathbb Z\), we must have
\[
\operatorname{col}\!\left(\prod p_i^{e_i}\right)\equiv \sum e_i t(p_i)\pmod r
\]
for some \(t(p_i)\in \mathbb Z/r\mathbb Z\), and for each prime \(p\), \(t(p)\) must be invertible mod \(r\) because \(1,p,\dots,p^{r-1}\) use all colours.

So each \(t(p)\) lies in \((\mathbb Z/r\mathbb Z)^\times\).

Now test \(n=pq\). Its divisors have colours
\[
0,\ t(p),\ t(q),\ t(p)+t(q)\pmod r.
\]
The four colours occurring among these divisors must have multiplicities differing by at most \(1\).

If \(r>4\), then all four must be distinct, otherwise one colour appears twice and many colours appear \(0\) times, giving difference \(2\), impossible. So for every distinct primes \(p,q\),
\[
0,\ t(p),\ t(q),\ t(p)+t(q)
\]
must be distinct mod \(r\).

In particular,
\[
t(p)\neq t(q),\qquad t(p)+t(q)\neq 0,\ t(p),\ t(q).
\]
Hence
\[
t(p)\neq t(q),\qquad t(p)\neq -t(q).
\]
So distinct primes must get distinct units, and no two can be negatives of each other.

But there are infinitely many primes and only finitely many residue classes mod \(r\), impossible.

Therefore \(r\le 4\).

---

## Step 3: Check \(r=2,3,4\)

### Case \(r=2\)

This is possible: colour \(n\) by the parity of \(\Omega(n)\), the total number of prime factors counted with multiplicity.

Then for divisors of any \(n\), exactly half (or differ by at most 1) have even \(\Omega\) and half odd. Indeed, if
\[
n=\prod p_i^{a_i},
\]
then divisors correspond to choices \(0\le e_i\le a_i\), and the parity of \(\sum e_i\) is equally distributed up to difference \(1\). So \(r=2\) works.

### Case \(r=3\)

Colour \(n\) by \(\Omega(n)\pmod 3\).

For \(n=\prod p_i^{a_i}\), divisors correspond to exponent vectors \((e_i)\), and colour is \(\sum e_i\pmod 3\). The number of such vectors in each residue class modulo \(3\) differs by at most \(1\). This follows because for each variable \(e_i\), the residues \(0,1,\dots,a_i\) are as evenly distributed mod \(3\) as possible, and convolution preserves the “difference at most 1” property. Hence \(r=3\) works.

### Case \(r=4\)

Similarly colour \(n\) by \(\Omega(n)\pmod 4\).

But \(n=pq\) has divisors \(1,p,q,pq\) with colours
\[
0,1,1,2 \pmod 4,
\]
so the colour counts are \(1,2,1,0\). The difference between colour \(1\) and colour \(4\) is \(2\), violating the condition.

So \(r=4\) does **not** work.

---

Thus only \(r=2,3\) are possible.

Final answer: \(\boxed{2,3}\)
